reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware interpretation of a venting-associated pressure transient versus later thermal runaway, and the correct options are tightly anchored to the described sequence (temperature blip, modest pressure rise, then runaway with extreme pressure). Decoys are largely single-fault and plausible, forcing experts to avoid common extrapolations (surface vs internal temperature, event misattribution, and overinterpreting cooling). Minor risk remains that some readers may debate whether venting gas release can be asserted as the sole cause of the intermediate pressure rise without additional diagnostic signals, slightly softening discriminative sharpness.

This short-answer item cleanly targets boundary-aware scale-transfer judgment: when small-n proportionality is admissible, what breaks it at module scale, and why a single-cell chemistry pressure ratio is not directly portable. It strongly discriminates experts who recognize interaction/confinement nonlinearity and evidence-base limits, though it risks some grading latitude because it’s open-ended and could permit partially correct responses that emphasize different (but still valid) limiting factors.
